Next-hop retrieval (priority order): 1) Fetch "Levitate" disambiguation or main page ("Levitate - Wikipedia") — inspect lede/infobox for listed album(s) and their "Genre" field; 2) For each candidate album page listed (e.g., "Levitate (artist) (album)") fetch the article lede/infobox and "Genre"/"Background"/"Release" sections; 3) If needed, run tight searches: 'Levitate album genre site:wikipedia.org' and '"Levitate" album genre' to locate non-Wikipedia sources or specific artist pages.

input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Priority rules for ambiguity and disambiguation:
- If summary_1 lists multiple candidate anchors or otherwise signals ambiguity, emit a single connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens (use the order or priority visible in summary_1) and then append exactly one short attribute token or a visible year token for disambiguation.
- If summary_1 contains a comma-separated list of individuals and the missing evidence concerns a member, prefer the earliest-listed individual token paired with the visible group token and append the single attribute token member.
- If the question contains a visible year token that disambiguates a title, include that year token before the attribute token in the query.
- If none of the above applies, emit a single-anchor query using the most relevant visible token and at most one short attribute token when the question implies an attribute.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.

retrieved_doc_snippets: Levite | A Levite (or Levi) is a Jewish male whose descent is traced by tradition to Levi. As a surname, Levite status may be indicated by the term "HaLevi", which consists of the Hebrew prefix "ה" "Ha-" ("the") plus "Levi" (Levite). The daughter of a Levite is a ""Bat Levi"" ("Bat" being Hebrew for "daughter").
King levitation | The King Rising levitation is a levitation illusion developed by and named after Corey King. Some consider it to be a variation of the Balducci levitation.

Levitation (paranormal) | Levitation or transvection in the paranormal context is the rising of a human body and other objects into the air by mystical means. Some parapsychology and religious believers interpret alleged instances of levitation as the result of supernatural action of psychic power or spiritual energy. The scientific community states there is no evidence that levitation exists and alleged levitation events are explainable by natural causes (such as magic trickery, illusion, and h ...[truncated]
Asrah levitation | The Asrah levitation, sometimes called Lighter than Air, is a classic levitation illusion.

Spin-stabilized magnetic levitation | Spin-stabilized magnetic levitation is a phenomenon of magnetic levitation whereby a spinning magnet or array of magnets is levitated via magnetic forces above another magnet or array of magnets, and stabilised by gyroscopic effect due to a spin that is neither too fast, nor too slow to allow for a necessary precession.

Aerodynamic levitation | Aerodynamic levitation is the use of gas pressure to levitate materials so that they are no longer in physical contact with any container. In scientific experiments this removes contamination and nucleation issues associated with physical contact with a container.

Levitation discography | Levitation were an English rock band fronted by ex-House of Love guitarist Terry Bickers. Levitation's music and attitude challenged an early 1990s UK alternative music scene dominated by shoegazing and Madchester.
source_state.summary_2: The provided passages do not state the genre of the album "Levitate". Levitation were an English rock band fronted by ex-House of Love guitarist Terry Bickers.

output.query: Levitate (The Fall album) The Fall (band) What genre does the album "Levitate" come from?

retrieved_doc_snippets: Levitate (The Fall album) | Levitate is an album (the 19th) by The Fall, released in 1997 on Artful Records. "Levitate" became the last album to feature two long-time Fall members, drummer Karl Burns and bass player Steve Hanley (whose playing was once described by Smith as the defining element of the group's music).

Levitate (Hollywood Undead song) | "Levitate (Digital Dog club mix)" is a song by American rock group Hollywood Undead and was released as the lead single from their first remix album, "American Tragedy Redux". It is the first song on the official track list and was added to rock radio formats on October 18, 2011. The song "Levitate" was originally featured on Hollywood Undead's latest studio album American Tragedy (album) but was not featured as a single. The remix of the song (the first versio ...[truncated]

Levitate (Bruce Hornsby album) | Levitate is the tenth studio album by Bruce Hornsby. It was Hornsby's third studio album with his touring band, Bruce Hornsby and the Noisemakers, and was his first release with Verve Records.

The Marshall Suite | The Marshall Suite is a 1999 album by The Fall (their 20th). The album builds on the techno-influenced beats of its predecessor "Levitate" (1997), while also returning to a more rockabilly-influenced sound reminiscent of earlier Fall line-ups. The end result has songs like the catchy "Touch Sensitive", and the strange, complex, thumping jungle beats of "Crying Marshall". The album was long out of print, but a new 3-disc edition was released in the summer of 2011.

Nest (band) | Nest is a neofolk / ambient band from Riihimäki, Finland, formed in 1999. The band's inspiration comes from the Finnish folklore, as well as from the works of J.R.R. Tolkien and the Grimm Brothers. Kantele, a traditional Finnish musical instrument, is very prominent in the band's music. They contributed a cover of the song "The Gallant Crow" to the Skepticism tribute album "Entering the Levitation".
Levitation (album) | Levitation is the tenth studio album by English rock group Hawkwind, released in 1980. It peaked at No. 21 on the UK Albums Chart.

Levitate (Hadouken! song) | "Levitate" is a song by the British band Hadouken! . It was released on 20 January 2013 as the fifth single from their third studio album "Every Weekend" (2013). It was produced by British duo Loadstar and Dutch bass music trio Noisia (under their Nightwatch alias). The song was made popular by the band's "People Are Awesome 2013" viral video that has accumulated over 120 million YouTube views.
